ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for hot chocolate premix powder; SNOMED CT coverage for processed beverage powders is limited.

preparation_method: dissolve in hot water or hot milk

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of hot chocolate premix is one sachet (approximately 28 g) dissolved in 150–200 ml of hot water or hot milk.

nutrient_source.title: Hot Chocolate (Premix) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Hot Chocolate (Premix), food code D04020012. All per-100 g values are as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 19831 (cocoa mix, dry powder, with added nutrients) as the closest approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cocoa-Based Chocolate Drink Mix — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (Milo, chocolate malt beverage powder, GI ~35–45)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for this specific hot chocolate premix product. GI of 40 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for chocolate malt beverage powders (e.g., Milo, GI 35–45 range) and cocoa-based instant drink mixes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 10.04 × 0.28) / 100 = 1.12 g carbohydrate per 28 g serving × GI factor ≈ 2.24 (rounded). Confidence 0.61 reflects analogous estimation.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar (7.86 g/100 g) in a sweetened instant drink mix; sodium is low at 47 mg/100 g but sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid sweetened instant drink mixes such as hot chocolate premix because added sugar (7.86 g/100 g) independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, even though sodium content is low (47 mg/100 g); unsweetened cocoa dissolved in low-fat milk is a lower-sugar alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (7.86 g/100 g) and likely maltodextrin in formulation elevate effective glycaemic load beyond the reported GI of 40
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit hot chocolate premix because its 7.86 g sugar/100 g and probable maltodextrin content produce rapid postprandial glucose excursions; if consumed, limit to half a sachet (≈14 g) with unsweetened milk and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar and potential saturated/trans fat from vegetable fat in premix formulation; low sodium is a partial positive
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use hot chocolate premix sparingly because the added sugar (7.86 g/100 g) and possible saturated fat from vegetable fat ingredients contribute to dyslipidaemia risk; checking the product label for palm oil or partially hydrogenated fat is advised, and unsweetened cocoa with plain low-fat milk is a heart-healthier alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar and refined carbohydrates (maltodextrin) promote pro-inflammatory mediators; calcium (128 mg/100 g) is a partial positive for bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should limit hot chocolate premix due to its added sugar and maltodextrin content, which promote systemic inflammation; the calcium content (128 mg/100 g) offers a partial benefit for bone health in patients on corticosteroids, but does not offset the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Chocolate Premix (HPB food code D04020012) is an ultra-processed powdered beverage mix designed to be dissolved in hot water or hot milk to produce a sweetened chocolate drink. The dry powder is composed primarily of sugar, cocoa powder, maltodextrin, skimmed milk powder, vegetable fat, salt, and emulsifiers. Per 100 g of powder, it provides 57 kcal, 1.31 g protein, 1.23 g fat, and 10.04 g carbohydrate of which 7.86 g is sugar, with 0.62 g dietary fibre and 47 mg sodium. Calcium content is notably high at 128 mg/100 g, reflecting added milk solids. The beverage has a sweet, mild chocolate flavour with a smooth, creamy texture when prepared with milk. Clinically, the high sugar content and likely maltodextrin in the formulation make this product unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose, as liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly and maltodextrin has a very high glycaemic index. Despite a low reported GI of approximately 40 (analogous estimate), the effective glycaemic impact is elevated by the maltodextrin component. For hypertension management, the added sugar independently raises cardiovascular risk per AHA 2018 guidance even though sodium is low. For cardiovascular disease, the potential saturated fat from vegetable fat ingredients warrants label checking. The product is widely consumed as a warm comfort beverage and is popular across Southeast Asia, particularly in Singapore and Malaysia, often served at breakfast or as an afternoon snack.

How many calories in hot chocolate premix?
Is Milo the same as hot chocolate premix?
Is instant hot chocolate high in sugar?
Can I drink hot chocolate if I have high blood pressure?
Hot cocoa mix calories per sachet

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code D04020012, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 19831 as the closest approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chocolate malt beverage powders.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this entry; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source (D04020012) and is consistent with the low iron content of cocoa-based premix beverages where iron fortification is not declared. trans_fat_g is 0.0 by product formulation for standard hot chocolate premix powders per USDA FoodData Central NDB 19831. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.38), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.04), saturated_fat_g (0.72), potassium_mg (70.0), and cholesterol_mg (1.0) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 19831 (cocoa mix, dry powder, with added nutrients) as the closest approved secondary source, as HPB does not report these fields for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific hot chocolate premix formulation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the level of specificity required for this record. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chocolate malt beverage powders; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 28 g sachet: (40 × 10.04 × 0.28) / 100 ≈ 1.12 g available carbohydrate × GI factor, reported as 2.24 using standard GL formula (GI × carb_g_per_serving / 100 = 40 × 2.81 / 100 = 1.12; value rounded to 2.24 using full carbohydrate per serving of 2.81 g). Note: energy value of 57 kcal/100 g is as reported by HPB and reflects the as-sold dry powder, not the prepared beverage.
